coredata.dc:description: Cellulose-based carbon dots (C-CDs) with fluorescence and non-toxicity properties have attracted wide attention in the field of biomedicine. However, constructing dual-stimuli hydrogels incorporated with C-CDs using biomass-based raw materials with biodegradable and antimicrobial characteristics for drug sustained-release remains a challenge. Herein, an interpenetrated-network method was proposed in which the N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) monomer, regarded as the second network, was polymerized via photo-initiation. Additionally, C-CDs derived from lychee waste were used as cross-linking agent, which was introduced into β-cyclodextrin (β-CD)/carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) first-network system, to fabricate a dual-network fluorescence hydrogels called β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM). The design of dual-network hydrogel can effectively enhance swelling and water retention abilities within a certain time. To evaluate the biomedical application of the obtained hydrogel, ibuprofen, as a poorly water-soluble model drug, was loaded into the hydrogel utilizing advantages of possessing hydrophobic inner cavity of β-CD and the highly dense structure of double-network hydrogels, to complete release behavior under different pH and temperature, with the maximum release rate reaching up to 96 %. Furthermore, it is noted that the cellulose-based interpenetrated-network hydrogel has access to cells but exhibits no cytotoxicity in vitro. Herein, this dual-responsive fluorescent hydrogel is expected to play a potential role in drug delivery and drug tracking.

(c) Results of CCK-8 assay on HepG2 cells after incubation with β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM). (d) In vitro antioxidant performance of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel against DPPH. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose-based carbon dots (C-CDs) with fluorescence and non-toxicity properties have attracted wide attention in the field of biomedicine. However, constructing dual-stimuli hydrogels incorporated with C-CDs using biomass-based raw materials with biodegradable and antimicrobial characteristics for drug sustained-release remains a challenge. Herein, an interpenetrated-network method was proposed in which the N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) monomer, regarded as the second network, was polymerized via photo-initiation. Additionally, C-CDs derived from lychee waste were used as cross-linking agent, which was introduced into β-cyclodextrin (β-CD)/carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) first-network system, to fabricate a dual-network fluorescence hydrogels called β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM). The design of dual-network hydrogel can effectively enhance swelling and water retention abilities within a certain time. To evaluate the biomedical application of the obtained hydrogel, ibuprofen, as a poorly water-soluble model drug, was loaded into the hydrogel utilizing advantages of possessing hydrophobic inner cavity of β-CD and the highly dense structure of double-network hydrogels, to complete release behavior under different pH and temperature, with the maximum release rate reaching up to 96 %. Furthermore, it is noted that the cellulose-based interpenetrated-network hydrogel has access to cells but exhibits no cytotoxicity in vitro. Herein, this dual-responsive fluorescent hydrogel is expected to play a potential role in drug delivery and drug tracking.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data supporting the findings of this study are available upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel, three-dimensional polymeric network structures, can absorb a large amount of water without getting dissolved, which is responsible for the formation of physical and chemical bonds among polymer chains of hydrogels [1]. Hydrogel has been widely applied in the biomedical field, especially as a kind of potential carrier for drug loading and delivery, taking advantage of its swelling ability, biocompatibility, and structural controllability. However, conventional crosslinking agents usually applied in the process of preparing hydrogels, including glutaraldehyde, epichlorohydrin, and other bifunctional materials, possess certain toxicity, which is inferior to carrying out cytotoxicity tests [2]. Herein, exploring a non-toxic crosslinking agent to synthesize the polymeric structure of hydrogel is still an approaching problem. Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, are distinguished by their superior antibacterial properties, excellent chemical stability, and environmentally friendly degradability [3,4]. Accordingly, their unique properties make them highly suitable for potential applications in the biomedical field. Furthermore, the surface of CDs is rich in reactive functional groups such as carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and amino (-NH₂) groups, which can interact with functional groups on polymer chains through covalent bonding, hydrogen bonding, or electrostatic interactions, thereby serving as crosslinking nodes within polymer networks [5]. Compared to traditional crosslinking agents, CDs derived from biomass carbon materials exhibit natural biocompatibility and low/non-toxicity, which is particularly advantageous for the development of drug delivery systems. Importantly, because of the presence of CDs, the efficiency of drug loading has an obvious increase by π-π interactions, and targeted drugs can be preciously delivered to specific cells, which reduces side effects and leakage of the drug in the human body [6].

Up to date, a series of hydrogels consisting of synthetic or natural polymer materials have been reported, among which polysaccharide-based hydrogels have gained particular attention due to their outstanding biodegradability, eco-friendly, similar living tissue, and ease of degradation under the physiological microenvironment [7,8]. Based on these unique characteristics, hydrogels obtained from abundant natural polymers are regarded as a suitable drug-loading platform for drug delivery [9–11]. Cellulose with availability, renewability, and rich on the earth becomes a promising candidate to substitute petroleum-based materials, which accelerates the development of cellulose in the preparation of hydrogels [12]. Unfortunately, cellulose with good hydrophilicity, so cellulose-based hydrogels have poor ability to deliver hydrophobic drugs. This, in essence, is the problem of incompatibility between the hydrophobic drugs and the hydrophilic polymer in the three-dimensional network of hydrogels, that is, cellulose-based hydrogels have difficulties in loading these hydrophobic drugs. Also, it is not proper to be chosen as hydrophobic drug carriers to achieve controlled delivery from these hydrogels [13].

In order to break this limitation of cellulose-based hydrogels, the introduction of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) becomes a major trend to synthesize cellulose-based hydrogels. The outer edge of β-CD is hydrophilic while the inner cavity is hydrophobic, which can provide hydrophobic binding sites and form host-guest inclusion complexes with hydrophobic drug molecules [14,15]. Therefore, the introduction of β-CD could improve drug loading capacity. Furthermore, due to the formation of β-CD complexes, the diffusion rate of the drugs presents a decreased trend, thus hydrophobic drugs can be released slowly and continuously. Binding is thought to be attributed to weak intermolecular forces and chemical bonds. The modification of β-CD can be conducted via hydroxyl groups including crosslinking, esterification, and reduction, resulting in a targeted product with specific properties [16,17]. Taking this into account, some researchers have designed β-CD-incorporated cellulose-based hydrogels for sustained hydrophobic drug delivery.

Compared to native cellulose, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), which is a cellulose derivative, performs better solubility and solvent adaptability [18,19]. It is noted that CMC exhibits pH sensitivity behavior due to the rich carboxyl groups on CMC chains through the gaining or losing of hydrogen protons [20]. CMC has anionic characters that allow it to bind positively charged materials like cationic polymers [21]. Hydrogels with excellent physicochemical properties can be designed by utilizing their -COOH and -OH functional groups. N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), with non-toxicity, solubility as well as mechanically strong, is a renowned temperature-responsive monomer that can be introduced into cellulose-based hydrogels, which endowed products with temperature sensitivity [22–24]. Taking good advantage of the inherent property of lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of the poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM), prepared hydrogels can achieve temperature-dependent controlled drug release in the human body. Therefore, dual-stimulus hydrogels are expected to be synthesized through a combination of CMC and PNIPAM in drug delivery [25]. There are many efforts devoted to hydrogel synthesis based on CMC and PNIPAM. Song et al. [1] successfully fabricated a dual-network hydrogel consisting of CMC, PNIPAM, and acrylamide to deliver a hydrophilic drug. Additionally, CMC and PNIPAM were used to prepare the dual-responsive aerogel via interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) technology for delivery of anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) [26]. However, the choices of crosslinking agent for the CMC and PNIPAM system are usually conventional small organic molecules, such as epichlorohydrin, that are toxic and there are still few studies on the preparation of hydrogels with fluorescence combined by CMC and NIPAM.

Though natural polymers are biocompatible and biodegradable, they also tend to the problem of low mechanical strength, as opposed to synthetic polymers [26–29]. Designing an interesting system to combine β-CD with other polymers can obtain high-strength hydrogels and improve the delivery of drugs. Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is a water-soluble polymer used as a reinforcing agent in biomedical applications, especially in the form of hydrogels [30]. Recently, the natural biomass materials with unique biological structure were successfully introduced into the three-dimensional network of PVA hydrogel, which possessed the excellent performance [31]. Because of the addition of PVA, various properties of cellulose-based hydrogels have a dramatic increase, including mechanical strength [32]. Yang et al. [33] developed a composite conductive organic hydrogel with high mechanical strength (2.2 MPa) using polyvinyl alcohol and o-carboxymethyl chitosan as raw materials, which was applied in human health monitoring. In addition, stable PVA can be prepared by both physical and chemical methods, thereby regulating its mechanical properties for the development of drug delivery carrier [34]. However, PVA hydrogels do not possess any sensitivity to external stimuli [35]. Therefore, it is crucial to introduce the PVA into those of other polymers to synthesize smart types of hydrogels that offer specific properties [36].

In this work, we propose a dual-network hydrogel composed of both natural (β-CD and CMC) and synthetic polymers (PVA and PNIPAM), leveraging the unique properties of each component. This design provides an effective platform with good biocompatibility, antibacterial activity, high drug-loading efficiency, and enhanced mechanical properties for the sustained release of ibuprofen, compared to using individual polymers. A targeted introduction of heat-responsive NIPAM in which the β-CD/CMC-PVA network is synthesized via free radical polymerization could enhance single-network swelling capability, meanwhile, the choice of a temperature-sensitive monomer can make complex hydrogel (β-CD/CMC-based (PVA-NIPAM)) dual response. Furthermore, phenylbis(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphine oxide was selected as the photoinitiator due to its stable photolysis byproducts, broad spectral response, and high initiation efficiency under UV irradiation, all of which are advantageous for the preparation of double-network biomass-based hydrogels via free radical polymerization. The use of carbon dots as crosslinkers, instead of conventional ones, helps avoid cytotoxicity in the drug delivery application. Moreover, their down-conversion properties endow the (β-CD/CMC-based (PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel with high fluorescence. Therefore, dual-network β-CD/CMC-based (PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel as a drug carrier is anticipated to play a critical role in stimulating the release behavior in the circumstances of human intestinal pH micro-environment and physiological temperature.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC; M. weight = 250,000 (DS = 0.7), Viscosity: 1500–3100 MPa·s), 2,2-Di (4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH; M. weight = 394.32 g/mol), and ibuprofen (M. weight = 206.28 g/mol) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology, China. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA; M. weight = 89,000–98,000, 99 + % hydrolyzed) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD; M. weight ==1135 g/mol), N-Isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM; M. weight = 113.16 g/mol), phenylbis(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphine oxide (M. weight = 418.47 g/mol) and melamine (MA) were purchased from Shanghai macklin biochemical Co., Ltd., China. All reagents used were of analytical grade unless special stated.

A one-step solvothermal approach was adopted to synthesize fluorescent CDs using lychee exocarp as a carbon source. 500 mg of lychee exocarp and 200 mg of MA (as a nitrogen source) were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon reactor and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. The reacted solution was collected after centrifugation at 11000 rpm for 15 min. To further remove any residual contaminants after centrifugation, the collected supernatant solution was filtered through a disposable 0.22 μm syringe filter made of polytetrafluoroethylene membrane. Following that, the obtained solutions were purified through dialysis in a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 1000 Da for 24 h. Finally, the obtained solution was kept at 4 °C for later use. The resulting solution was called MA-CDs. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C after filtration to obtain solid-state CDs for the following characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based hydrogels and β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based PVA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The β-Cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based PVA hydrogels were designed using polymer solution reactions. 2 g β-CD, 5 g CMC, and citric acid (10 % -15 % of polymer amount) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water at 40 °C and continuously stirred at 500 rpm. Citric acid served as a crosslinking agent in the preparation of hydrogels, that is, this anhydride reacts with the hydroxyl group on a nearby component (β-CD or CMC) to form an ester crosslinked. Afterward, 4 g PVA was dissolved in 40 mL distilled water at 85 °C and added to the above mixture of β-CD/CMC solution. Then, precursors were placed in a round-bottom flask thermally crosslinked for 8 h at 90 °C in an oil bath to form a sticky solution, and the β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogels were obtained after freeze-drying for 48 h [13].
The preparation of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based hydrogels was the same as that of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based PVA hydrogels except adding PVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based hydrogels β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based (PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: 4 g NIPAM was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water at room temperature. Next, 10 mL MA-CD (10 mg/mL) served as a cross-linker and 2 % (relative to monomer mass) of photoinitiator (phenylbis (2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphine oxide) was added into the NIPAM solution. The mixture was stirred at 800 rpm until the solid disappeared to form a clear solution in cold hydrazine at low temperature, and the obtained solution was called prepolymer solution (the entire procedure was carried out at the pH 6.0 ± 0.2). Prepolymer solution was obtained, and then carried out photoinitiation under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp (λ = 365 nm). β-CD/CMC-based PVA gel was immersed in the prepolymer solution at the temperature of −6 °C for 24 h. Then the β-CD/CMC-based PVA gel containing NIPAM monomer molecules was placed in a glass bottle under a nitrogen atmosphere for photoinitiation for 1 h. The ultraviolet curing light source has a wavelength of 365 nm (ultraviolet maximum absorption wavelength of phenylbis (2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphine oxide is 350 nm) and a power of 50 W. The distance from the upper surface of the hydrogel to the lamp tube is 40 cm [1]. Once the polymerization reaction was completed, the cylindrical hydrogels were taken out of the glass molds to place them into a freeze dryer. Finally, the β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based (PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel discs were collected when they reached a constant weight. The obtained dry hydrogel discs were stored in a vacuum desiccator until further studies were carried out.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Tensor 27, Bruker, German) was used to characterize the types of chemical bonds in hydrogels, ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH STA 449 F5, Germany) was used to characterize thermal stability of hydrogel samples. The hydrogels were heated from 60 °C to 700 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. To verify the temperature of the maximum rate of weight loss, a derivative TG (DTG) curve was performed. The crystallinity of the synthesized carbon dots and hydrogels was displayed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (MiniFlex600, Japan) with Cu-Ka radiation between 5° and 90°. We used field emission scanning electron microscope (model Supra 55 Sapphire) to analyze the surface morphology of β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA, and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-co-NIPAM) hydrogels after freeze-drying. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were carried out on a JEOL JEM-F200 with at 200 kV operating voltage to warrant the distribution of MA-CDs. Fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained on a FS5 spectrofluorometer. A UV-8000 T UV–visable spectrophotometer (Metash, China) was used to display UV–vis spectra. Zeta potential and nano-particle size were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK). An ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer microprobe was used to take on XPS spectra (Thermo Scientific Nexsa, USA). Solid 13C magic angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (13C MAS) NMR spectra of β-CD, CMC, PVA, and the β-CD/CMC-PVA gel were measured using JEOL JNM-ECZ600R spectrometer operating at 600 MHz. Chemical shifts were calibrated with adamantane. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy was performed using a 600 MHz Bruker Avance NEO 600 (Bruker, Germany) spectrometer. The rheological properties of the hydrogels were tested by using a Haake Mars60 rheometer with a diameter of 20 mm at room temperature.

The compression test was performed on the universal tensile machine (CMT 6103), and the pressure plate was used in the tensile machine. The specimens used in the compression experiment were cut into a cylinder with a diameter of 20 mm and a height of 17 mm, and the compression rate was set at 2 mm/min. Each hydrogel was repeated three times.

To evaluate the swelling rate and maximum swelling rate of designed hydrogels, the prepared samples were initially subjected to a solvent replacement with deionized water to ensure the removal of unreacted monomers or other impurities. The test methods of swelling rate and maximum swelling rate of hydrogels are estimated: prepared hydrogels after solvent replacement were freeze-dried and they were cut into several pieces with the same mass. Then, these samples were dipped into deionized water for swelling. Took these gels every 30 min, wiped off the superficial water with filter paper, and weighed it to get the swollen weight. The swelling rate of hydrogels is calculated by the following formula:
(1)
Swelling ratio
%
=
W t
−
W d
W d
× 100
where Wd and Wt represent the weight of the initial dry sample and the weight of the swollen sample at different time intervals, respectively. Furthermore, the balanced swelling hydrogels can be freeze-dried repeatedly, and then placed in a vacuum drying oven to dry reach constant weights. Re-swelling tests can be carried out to characterize the re-swelling performance of these hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and temperature sensitivity of β-cyclodextrin/carboxymethyl cellulose-based (PVA-NIPAM) gel
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-responsive swelling behavior of hydrogel samples is estimated by the alteration of swelling rate and volume under different pH values (2−11) at the temperature of 25 °C. The prepared hydrogels after the solvent replacement were immersed in phosphate buffers (PBS) with different pH values and recorded the changes in swelling volume and swelling rate after soaking 6 h. The thermal-responsive experiments, on the other hand, were conducted in a water bath, and differences in sample volume were observed with the change of temperature rising from 10 °C to 50 °C.

Three pieces of 10 mg of dried hydrogel samples, that is β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA, β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM), were immersed into 5 mg/mL ibuprofen solution to diffuse and permeate ibuprofen under neutral conditions, respectively The encapsulating efficiency of ibuprofen can be calculated by measuring the concentration of ibuprofen in the unloading solution using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Ibuprofen release behavior from β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA, and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels were performed under the same experimental conditions. The release behavior of ibuprofen at 25 °C and 37 °C was carried out in PBS buffer solution with pH = 7.4. At specified time intervals, 2 mL of the solution was withdrawn (replaced with a 2 mL fresh PBS buffer) and measured its concentration using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Meanwhile, the release behavior of ibuprofen at pH = 1.2, 5.0, and 7.4 was carried out at 25 °C. Relying on the expected maximum peak value, the absorbance of the solution was monitored at 222 nm for ibuprofen with a UV–vis spectrophotometer to calculate the drug concentration.
(2)
Encapsulating efficiency
%
=
Q 0
−
Q r
/
Q 0
× 100
(3)

the amount of released drug the amount of loaded drug

Where
Q 0
= the total amount of drug initially added;
Q r
= the amount of free (unencapsulated) drug remaining in the solution after encapsulation.

The gel fraction (GF%) was measured by first weighing a hydrogel sample that had been dried in an oven at 37 °C for 48 h (
M i
). After this initial drying period, the sample was immersed in distilled water for 2 days. Following the immersion, the sample was dried again at 37 °C until a constant weight (
M f
) was achieved. The GF% was then calculated using the following formula:
(4)

The GF% was determined for HPC-coCMC/PNIPAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and antioxidant activity evaluation of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The tests of cell viability carried out on different hydrogels were determined by CCK-8 assay. Also, the effect of hydrogel on HepG2 cell survival was detected by Live/dead kit assay. Briefly, HepG2 cells provided by iCell Bioscience Inc., Shanghai, were cultured with MEM media containing fetal bovine serum with a ratio of 9:1. A total of 8 × 103 cells/mL (HepG2 cells) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere to facilitate cell attachment to the wall. After 24 h, the cells were cleaned with PBS three times and added culture medium containing 10 % CCK-8 culturing in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 2 h. The absorbance was recorded at 450 nm by a multimode reader (SPARK 10 M). Tests were repeated three times for each group. The cell adhesion and survival rate were observed by confocal laser microscopy (FV1200, Olympus). The antioxidant efficiency of hydrogels loaded ibuprofen was performed using the approach of scavenging the stable 1, 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical [37,38].

Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the prepared hydrogel [39,40]. E. coli or S. aureus was cultured in Luria-Bertani broth (LB), which was sterilized for 15 min using a high temperature and pressure steam sterilization pot at 121 °C. Before bacterial inoculation, the bacterial culture tube without the sample (as a control group), β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gels were sterilized in the condition of high temperature and pressure steam sterilization at a temperature of 121 °C for 20 min. After that, 60 mg of the samples were weighed and the bacterial solution was diluted to 106 CFU/mL with aseptic PBS. Next, 2 mL of diluted bacterial solution was added according to the groups and then placed in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for static cultivation. Co-culture was performed for 24 h and 48 h, after which the samples were diluted further. Subsequently, 100 μL of bacterial suspension with the dilution ratios of 1:100 was uniformly coated onto the LB and cultured for another 18 h. Finally, photographs were taken to record the number of colonies.

The preparation process of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel is shown in Fig. 1 . β-CD/CMC based-PVA single network hydrogel first synthesized may involve grafting reaction and interpolymer crosslinking using citric acid (CA) as the cross-linking agent. When the amount of CA is 10 %, the β-CD/CMC based-PVA with maximum swelling capacity within 50 h could be obtained. Therefore, the introduction of CA could enhance the swellability as well as drug loading capability of the first network. In addition, the CMC endows the complex hydrogels with pH sensitivity, which is conducive to controlled release. After immersing β-CD/CMC based-PVA gel in the prepolymer solution containing NIPAM (monomer), MA-CDs (crosslinking agent), and Phenylbis (2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphine oxide as initiator, followed by UV-lamp photoinitiation, the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel can be prepared. CDs typically possess various functional groups on their surfaces, such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups. Silicon-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method and subsequently chemically crosslinked with carboxymethyl cellulose through amide bonds to form a gel material [41]. Chen et al. [42] used nitrogen-doped CDs as a crosslinking agent to prepare cellulose nanofibril (CNF) and chitosan (CS) based interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) hydrogel. Similarly, Lv et al. [43] used CDs and introduced them into the CMC-based hydrogel to prepare a novel fluorescent cellulose-based hydrogel. Therefore, CDs have the potential to serve as novel, non-toxic fluorescent crosslinkers for chemically cross-linked hydrogels [44–47]. During the polymerization process, the photoinitiator initiates the production of free radicals in NIPAM monomers by absorbing light energy. These new free radicals can continue to react with other NIPAM monomers, promoting chain extension and polymerization, which facilitates the formation of long-chain polymers. Furthermore, these free radicals could attack the amino groups (−NH2) on the surface of CDs, triggering a free radical transfer reaction that generates nitrogen-centered radicals (-NH•). These nitrogen-centered radicals then react with the CC double bonds in NIPAM monomers, forming new CN single bonds [48]. Subsequently, these newly formed free radicals can participate in chain propagation or crosslinking, thereby constructing a three-dimensional network structure (Fig. S1). Furthermore, when the temperature is below LCST, the amide groups of PNIPAM can bind to water molecules via hydrogen bonding, causing the polymer chains of the hydrogels to stretch and increasing the hydrophilicity of hydrogels [49]. In the stock solution of CDs solution, the carbonyl groups in the PNIPAM chains can interact with the amino groups/carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs via hydrogen bonding, which is also conducive to produce a network polymer. However, too little or too much cross-linking agent cannot make the hydrogel form a uniform pore structure. 10 mg/mL CDs solution is proper to cross-link the second network, making the hybrid hydrogel fluoresce and temperature response.

On the other hand, to explore numerous chemical groups and linkages that existed in prepared MA-CDs, an FT-IR spectrum was carried out as shown in Fig. 2(d). Stretching frequencies of 3450 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 clearly indicated the presence of O-H/ NH and C-OH, which endows CDs with good solubility. The stretching vibration band of CN was observed at 2183 cm−1 and the absorption peaks at 1103 cm−1, 1535 cm−1, and 1648 cm−1 can be attributed to -OH, CN, and CO contraction [51]. Based on these chemical bonds, we can conclude that N elements have been successfully doped onto the surface of CDs.

We also found that there was a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 20°-23° in the XRD image, illustrating highly disordered carbon atoms and a phase of amorphous carbon (Fig. 2(e)) [52]. Fig. 2(f) provided detailed emission spectra of melamine-doped CDs at different excitation wavelengths and revealed FL emission spectra were excitation-dependent. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission spectrum of melamine-doped CDs showed a red shift phenomenon [53]. Furthermore, to comprehensively analyze the optical properties of prepared CDs, the UV–vis spectrum and FL spectra of MA-CDs (liquid state) were recorded in Fig. 2(g). At the maximum excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the corresponding optimal emission wavelengths appeared at 450 nm, showing a typical blue luminescence for MA-CDs when exposed to UV light. In Fig. S2(a), the coordinate of MA-CDs in CIE was presented, proving that it displayed emission color consistent with PL results. From Fig. S2(b), there was a peak at 20–40 ppm attributed to aliphatic carbon atoms with sp3 hybridization; aliphatic carbon atoms were observed between 60 and 80 ppm, suggesting that they were related to N and O atoms with negatively charged. Meanwhile, a peak of 170 ppm was proved that C atoms in carboxyl or amide groups with sp2 hybridization [12].

We further evaluated the pH stability of these as-prepared CDs. The pH was adjusted to 2.0 by the slow addition of aqueous hydrochloric acid solution (0.1 M HCl), while the pH was increased to 12.0 by the slow addition of sodium hydroxide solution (0.1 M NaOH) [54,55]. The pH-dependent FL spectrum of N-doped CDs was shown in Fig. 2(h). The results showed that the fluorescence emission intensity of MA-CDs remained basically unchanged across different pH levels. That is to say, MA-CDs could maintain stable fluorescence emission in both strongly acidic and strongly basic solutions. The excellent pH stability could be attributed to the conjugate effect between the functional groups and carbon cores, inhibiting the protonation-deprotonation process. This result is consistent with previously reported literature [56–58]. In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was used to characterize the fluorescence properties of CDs. It was found that the QY of MA-doped dispersed in distilled water was 6.74 %.

The full XPS spectra of MA-CDs presented in Fig. 3(a) performed four peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.1 eV, and 532.4 eV approximately, suggesting that C, N, and O were included in prepared CDs. It can be concluded that the nitrogen percentage of MA-CDs is 3.58 %, which was higher than that of N-doped free CDs (2.9 %). From the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(b)), there were three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.1 eV, which were attributed to CC, CO, CO bonds, respectively [55]. In Fig. 3(c) high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s displayed two peaks, centered at approximately 399.2 eV and 399.9 eV, corresponding to CN and NH bonds, respectively. Furthermore, NO (531.1 eV) and CO (532.3 eV) were presented in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)). These results suggested that the -NH2 group was attached to the surface of CDs successfully.

Three types of hydrogel were immersed in distilled water at room temperature for a certain time (Fig. 4(a)). After freeze-drying, the surface morphology of the synthesized hydrogels was examined using SEM technology in Fig. 4(b-d). It is obvious that the morphology of β-CD/CMC hydrogels exhibited the micro- and macroporous structure, that is, cross-linked structure was apparent. This is due to the free water molecules in the cross-linked structure hydrogel being frozen and sublimed during the lyophilization process, creating voids in the structure. However, large cracks, irregular pores, and thin walls of β-CD/CMC hydrogels may be a major reason that resulted in low swelling capacity. Because of the addition of PVA, the surface of the β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogel is more regular and hard, and has more uniform porosities and folds compared to β-CD/CMC hydrogel, leading to the better swelling ability to keep stable in the application of biomedicine. In comparison to β-CD/CMC and β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogels, the introduction of dual-network structure can significantly enhance the cross-linking density of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel and presented a more compact arrangement of its internal pore structures. Statistical analysis revealed that the average pore size of the single-network β-CD/CMC hydrogel is 14.2 ± 9.1 μm (Fig. 4(e)). Upon incorporation of PVA, the pore size of the resulting β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogel decreased to 1.4 ± 0.2 μm, indicating a contraction in the pore structure (Fig. 4(f)). This reduction suggests that PVA interacts with the β-CD/CMC network, enhancing polymer chain entanglements and contributing to a more compact and structurally stable hydrogel network. After the incorporation of the thermoresponsive polymer PNIPAM and the cross-linking agent MA-CDs, the pore size of the composite hydrogel β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) significantly decreases to 0.4 ± 0.1 μm (Fig. 4(g)). This pronounced reduction is likely attributed to the formation of additional cross-linking points among polymer chain segments, which is facilitated by the multiple reactive sites existing on the surfaces of MA-CDs and the monomer NIPAM. As a result, the overall cross-linking density of the hydrogel network is enhanced. This phenomenon was consistent with the results reported in the literature [59]. Additionally, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms technique was used to evaluate the specific surface area and pore characteristic of synthesized hydrogels. As exhibited in Table 1 , the BET surface area, pore volume, and average pore size of dual-network hydrogel are 3.6 m2/g, 0.0030 cm3/g, and 10.8 nm, respectively. Notably, the specific surface area and pore volume of the double-network gel are smaller than those of the single-network gel. This phenomenon may be attributed to the incorporation of the second network, which results in a denser and more compact hydrogel structure. As a result, some small pores may collapse or merge into fewer ones, thereby reducing the specific surface area and pore volume. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S3(a) and (b), the pore size distribution of β-CD/CMC-PVA and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gels is in the range of 1.5–21.5 nm, indicating that the prepared hydrogels possess typical mesoporous characteristics and exhibit a relatively uniform pore size distribution. This is consistent with previous literature [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, FL and 13C NMR spectra analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the changes in surface functional groups, FT-IR technology was employed, with the resulting spectra displayed in Fig. 5(a). OH and NH stretching vibrations were exhibited around 3300 cm−1 appeared in all of the samples. The CH stretching vibration band was found at 2917 cm−1. The spectra of three hydrogels presented characteristic peaks at 1720 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1 the former suggesting CO stretching of carboxylic acid and ester groups, while the latter displaying CO stretching of the carboxylate group of CMC. For PVA, there is an evident peak occurring at 1151 cm−1, which was attributed to CO stretching vibration. Notably, the synthesized copolymers' spectra displayed two prominent peaks at around 1665 and 1535 cm−1 were corresponded to the adsorption of CO stretching and NH bending vibrations [61], illustrating that the second network of PNIPAM has crosslinked with the β-CD/CMC-PVA network successfully. Because of the presence of MA-CDs, 1595 cm−1 and 1203 cm−1 were observed to represent the bending vibration of -NH2 and CN stretching vibration, respectively. From these results, β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels based on MA-CDs as a cross-linker agent have been synthesized. Furthermore, these functional groups provided convenience for drug loading and delivery. From the PL spectra of MA-CDs and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM), they are evident to prove the presence of MA-CDs in the hybrid hydrogel (Fig. 5(b)). In comparison to the MA-CDs spectrum, the maximum emission wavelength of the dual-network hydrogel exhibited a blue shift. This shift is likely due to the formation of amide bonds between CDs and polymeric materials, along with hydrogen bonding interactions between the carbonyl groups in the PNIPAM chains and the amino groups or carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs within the hydrogel [62]. Meanwhile, it could also be proved that carbon dots can be regarded as a kind of crosslinking agent. Importantly, the three-dimensional network encapsulating MA-CDs resulted in a decrease in luminescence intensity for the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel. This reduction can be attributed to several factors, including inner photoluminescence reabsorption, filtering effects, and partial quenching, all of which are evident in the PL spectrum of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) [63]. The incorporation of MA-CDs endowed the hydrogel with fluorescent properties, opening up opportunities for the application of the prepared biomass-based hydrogels in the field of fluorescent bioimaging.

The 13C NMR spectra of β-CD, PVA, CMC, and β-CD/CMC-PVA gel are shown in Fig. S6(a). The carbon atoms of β-CD and CMC were distinguished symbolically. The spectrum of β-CD displayed sharp resonance peaks in the ranges of 101–104 ppm (C1), 72–85 ppm (C2-C5), and 58–64 ppm (C6). Also. The resonance peaks corresponding to the carbon atoms of CMC were observed in the ranges of 56–66 ppm (C6), 68–80 ppm (C2, 3, 5, 7), 81–89 ppm (C4), 98–109 ppm (C1), and 175–183 ppm (carboxyl carbon of the carboxylate group) [64]. The 13C NMR of pure PVA revealed a signal for the CH carbon characterized by three splitting peaks: the most downfield peak (Peak I) at 76.12 ppm, the central peak (Peak II) at 70.88 ppm, and the most upfield peak (Peak III) at 65.13 ppm [65]. The spectrum of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel presented the presence of all the CMC. Additionally, the peak associated with the carbonyl carbon of COO− exhibited broadening, which may be attributed to the presence of free acid (-COOH) and ester crosslinks within the composite hydrogels. The resonance peak corresponding to C1 of CMC exhibited a slight shift from 104.12 ppm to 103.84 ppm, which may be attributed to the interference from the β-CD peaks at 103.20, 102.31, and 101.72 ppm. Also, due to the crosslinking reaction with CMC, the intensity of the characteristic peak of PVA (Peak II) was found to be decreased. Fig. S6(b) shows the 1H NMR spectrum of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel. The peak at 5.13 ppm was attributed to the hydrogen belonging to the anomeric C of the cyclodextrin, and the peak of 4.1 ppm was related to -CH- peak of PVA. Furthermore, the peak at 11.9 ppm corresponded to the hydrogen associated with the carboxyl group of CMC. Importantly, the peak of 3.8–3.9 ppm was the hydrogen peak of -CH, which was coupled to the two -CH3 and was the characteristic peak of the isopropyl group [20]. Therefore, the NMR spectrum is consistent with the above results, that is, PNIPAM was successfully added to the β-CD/CMC-PVA polymeric network.

Thermogravimetric analysis is an important index for evaluating the thermodynamic stability and exploring the possible aggregate structure of materials. Fig. 5(c) presented the results of thermodynamic analysis. It can be seen from the DTG curves (Fig. 5(d)) that the decomposition temperature of the double-network hydrogel is about 6 °C higher than that of β-CD/CMC-PVA gels, suggesting that the formation of the double network does not alter the original structure of the β-CD/CMC-PVA gel and also indicates that there is no chemical cross-linking between the two network layers. Furthermore, the decomposition temperature of the β-CD/CMC gel is about 10 °C lower than that of the dual-network β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel [1]. These data could prove that the thermal stability of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel is much better than that of the other two types of hydrogel, which may be ascribed to the H-bonds or other intermolecular forces between β-CD/CMC or β-CD/CMC-PVA and PNIPAM in the dual-network hydrogel system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling-deswelling studies, assessment of the gel mass fraction, compressive strength and rheological properties of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The changes of swelling rate with the increase of swelling time for three types of hydrogels were evaluated at room temperature in distilled water. The results (Fig. 6(a)) were recorded that the maximum swelling rates of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel (42 g/g) and β-CD/CMC-PVA gel (26 g/g) are dramatically increased compared with single-network structure β-CD/CMC gel (12 g/g). For the β-CD/CMC hydrogel, the absence of PVA results in a relatively fragile network structure that lacks flexible chain segments and structural adjustability, making it difficult for water molecules to penetrate and leading to the lowest swelling capacity. On the other hand, the major reasons for faster diffusion of water and the greater change of swelling volume in the dual-network hydrogel are as followed: (i) the increased proportion of hydrophilic groups in the gel system attributed to free radical polymerization of PNIPAM, (ii) the construction of the dual-network structure makes the hydrogel own more uniform macroporous structure, (iii) many isolation points formed between the -COO− and -OH− of the β-CD/CMC-PVA gel the construction of the dual-network side chain by the introduction of the second layer of cationic molecules, which reduced the electrostatic repulsion between them. To further explore whether the interaction between β-CD and CMC/PVA affected the swelling behavior of the hydrogel, the swelling behavior of CMC/PVA hydrogels without β-CD was tested (Fig. S4(a)). The results showed that, compared to the hydrogel with β-CD added, the hydrogel without β-CD exhibited a more relaxed network structure and higher water permeability, leading to an increased swelling rate. The pH and temperature responsiveness of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) were examined by observing the changes in volume and swelling rate, with the results presented in Fig. 6(b-c). It is evident that β-CD/CMC and β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogels do not display temperature-responsive properties. However, compared to the β-CD/CMC gel, the higher swelling rate observed in β-CD/CMC-PVA gels is attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity of the network, which results from the incorporation of PVA chains that facilitate water uptake and network expansion [66]. Notably, incorporating PNIPAM into the dual-network β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel endows it with temperature sensitivity, as demonstrated by a pronounced increase in the swelling ratio between 20 °C and 30 °C. This is because, as the temperature increases, the mobility of the polymer chains is enhanced, allowing the network structure of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel to stretch and expand more flexibly. This increase in flexibility enhances the hydrogels' ability to absorb water. Another possible reason is that the increase in temperature affects the hydrogen bonding interactions between water molecules and the polymer chains [67,68]. At higher temperatures, some hydrogen bonds may be disrupted, leading to an increase in the distance between polymer chains [69]. This allows water molecules to penetrate more easily and occupy the voids within the polymer network, thereby increasing the swelling rate. When the temperature is in the range of 30 °C–37 °C, the hydrogel's swelling rate undergoes a stable increase (38 g/g–42 g/g). However, when the temperature exceeded 37 °C, the hydrogel started to shrink and finally collapsed at 40 °C. Additionally, we can conclude that there are obviously differences in the volume change of the three types of hydrogels when they swelled in different pH conditions within certain hours (pH: 2–11). When the pH is below 5, the swelling rate of the prepared hydrogels is relatively low (less than 20 g/g) and the swelling volume of the prepared hydrogels appears a small float-up. However, with the increase of pH from 5 to 7, there is a dramatic increase in swelling volume, reaching up to 12 g/g, 26 g/g, and 42 g/g, which corresponds to β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM), respectively, with gel volumes of 3, 5, and 8 times than that of the original volume of β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM). Therefore, the prepared hydrogel has the property of pH response in swelling studies. Besides, the swelling volume of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) is a slight decrease after 8 cycles (Fig. 6(d)), proving that the higher mechanical strength of the dual-network hydrogel than that of the other two hydrogels.

Apart from the temperature-, pH-dependent swelling characterizations, the deswelling behavior of the hydrogels was also displayed and the deswelling profiles of the obtained hydrogels at the temperature of 45 °C were presented in Fig. S4(b). Once the hydrogels after swelling in oven at 45 °C, all the swollen hydrogels became shrunk gradually and the water retention of the dual-network hydrogel was higher than that of single-network hydrogels greatly. It took 330 min to lose 90 %, 80 %, and 70 % of water for β-CD/CMC gel, β-CD/CMC-PVA gel, β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel, respectively. This phenomenon may be attributed to two reasons as followed: (i) three dry gels with the same quality have the highest swelling rate in the dual-network hydrogel related to the increased proportion of hydrophilic groups, which could increase the capability of water retention; (ii) more cross-linked sites formed between MA-CDs and NIPAM by the introduction of the second network structure, which retarded rate the water loss for β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM).

Furthermore, the gel mass fraction of hydrogels is a crucial parameter as it reflects the stability and integrity of the network structure and reveals the degradation behavior of the synthesized composite hydrogels in water (Fig. S4(c)). The results show that all three hydrogels exhibited less than 40 % decrease in mass loss when exposed to water for 48 h. The highest gelation was attributed to dual-network β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel (88.7 ± 4.4 %), while gelation was the lowest for β-CD/CMC gel (59.9 ± 3.0 %). Compared to β-CD/CMC gel, the gel fraction of β-CD/CMC-PVA gel showed an obvious increase at 78.2 ± 2.3 %, which could be due to enhanced chemical cross-linking. Additionally, the reduced degradation observed in the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel may generate more chain entanglement, leading to more crosslinking points and thereby improving gel mass fraction value. Therefore, the introduction of a second network creates more crosslinking points and forms a denser gel network, which will increase the gel fraction value. To further demonstrate the role of MA-CDs in hydrogels, β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel without MA-CDs was designed as a contrast for the gel fraction test. The gel mass fraction of the dual-network β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel without MA-CDs showed 81.3 ± 2.6 %, which was lower than that of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels crosslinked with MA-CDs. This is because MA-CDs, acting as crosslinking agents, provide more crosslinking sites, resulting in the formation of additional chemical crosslinks between the polymers and leading to a tighter three-dimensional network structure.

In the compressibility test, the double-network hydrogel exhibited a compressibility of 0.6 MPa, whereas the β-CD/CMC gel showed only 0.012 MPa, which was 50 times lower than that of the double-network hydrogel, as illustrated in Fig. S5(a). The superior compressive strength of double-network hydrogels arises from a synergistic mechanism in which the first, brittle network sacrifices itself to dissipate energy, while the second, flexible network maintains the overall structural integrity, achieving an ideal balance between high strength and toughness [70,71]. Consequently, the construction of a dual-network structure is good for the generation of uniform pore structure. Besides, the influence of MA-CDs on dual-network hydrogels should not be overlooked. Therefore, the shear stress of β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel was assessed via rheological experiments (Fig. S5(b)). It is obvious that the hydrogel crosslinked with MA-CDs can withstand higher shear stress compared to β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) without MA-CDs. This is because MA-CDs, acting as a crosslinking agent, can form a three-dimensional network structure in the hydrogel, which enhances the mechanical strength and stability of the dual-network β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel. Additionally, viscoelasticity is also a characteristic mechanical property of hydrogels, which reflects the energy dissipation and storage capacity of hydrogels. Fig. S5(c) showed that the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G") of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel crosslinked with MA-CDs. The result exhibited that the storage modulus of the hydrogel was higher than the loss modulus, and G' showed a relatively stable value in the detection frequency range, indicating that β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels had elastic, solid-like characteristics and stable mechanical properties. Furthermore, an alternate step stress experiment was also carried out as shown in Fig. S5(d). Alternating stress of 1 % and 100 % were applied to the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel every 200 s. After 5 cycles, the hydrogel almost recovered its initial modulus. Rheological tests showed that β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel has good deformation recovery performance owing to the existence of pore structures of different sizes in the hydrogel network structure.

The definition of encapsulation efficiency is the actual fraction of drug encapsulated in the drug-loaded hydrogel, which is an important parameter for a controlled drug release system. The percentage encapsulation efficiency of the hydrogel displayed ranged from 65 % to 89 %, as shown in Table S1, with the increase in the number of polymers in the hydrogel network. The lowest encapsulation efficiency with the least number of polymers that are CMC and β-CD can be explained due to the formation of a relatively loose hydrogel structure. In contrast, β-CD/CMC-PVA gels exhibited an increasing trend in drug loading, which can be explained by their higher swelling ratio and improved mechanical strength. Furthermore, a hydrogel with the dual network, that is β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel, formed a more rigid and dense structure, reducing the possibility of drug molecules leaching from the matrix, thus the encapsulation efficiency was up to the highest (89 %).

Besides, the function of β-CD in complex hydrogels for the loading of hydrophobic drugs has also been investigated (Table S1). The hydrogel composed of CMC and PVA has an encapsulation efficiency of only 52 %, which is a reduction of 13 % compared to the conditions with β-CD. Additionally, the drug encapsulation efficiency of the double-network hydrogel prepared without β-CD is similar to that of the β-CD/CMC-PVA gel, which is still significantly lower than that of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel (89 %). This is because there are more hydrogen bonding between the polar functional groups of drug molecules and the hydroxyl groups of β-CD, significantly increasing their encapsulation efficiency. The introduction of β-CD may alter the microstructure of the hydrogel, resulting in a more uniform network structure, which further enhances the drug encapsulation efficiency. This conclusion is consistent with Fig. S4(a). Although the β-CD/CMC-PVA hydrogel exhibits a lower swelling ratio compared to the CMC/PVA hydrogel, it effectively enhances the drug encapsulating capacity due to the inclusion interaction between β-CD and ibuprofen. The interaction between ibuprofen and β-CD enables the hydrogel to achieve high drug loading even under low swelling conditions. Therefore, β-CD plays an important role in improving the loading capability of hydrophobic drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1H NMR study of the hydrogel/ibuprofen complex and controlled release of ibuprofen from the hydrogels at different pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel, a kind of high-water adsorption and retention material, can be used as a carrier to load and deliver drugs. Ibuprofen was loaded on β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel with the highest maximum swelling rate (42 g/g) and released under different pH and temperature conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 (a–b), the double-network hydrogel exhibited a slower drug release rate and a lower cumulative release (51 %) compared to single-network hydrogels at a temperature of 25 °C. This phenomenon may be attributed to its denser internal structure and higher crosslinking density, which collectively restrict the diffusion of drug molecules [72]. Compared to the drug release rate from the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel at 25 °C, the drug release rate was improved at the temperature of 37 °C (96 %), indicating that the drug can be completely released from the collapsed hydrogel when the temperature higher than LCST. In contrast, the hydrogel without NIPAM showed no significant change in drug release rate upon increasing the temperature. This indicates that the double-network hydrogel possesses temperature-sensitive responsiveness. At the same time, we also tested the release of ibuprofen from the hydrogel matrix without β-CD under the optimal condition (pH = 7.4, T = 37 °C) to verify whether the hydrogel's burst release behavior for this hydrophobic drug could be avoided by applying the β-CD. Its release profile can be explained by taking into account the presence of a host-guest interaction with the β-CD. Ibuprofen release from hydrogels with β-CD occurs without any burst, as shown in Fig. 7(b). In contrast to these results, single network (CMC/PVA) and dual network (CMC/PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels, which are unable to form inclusion complexes, released the ibuprofen much faster: the value of ibuprofen burst release in the case of hydrogels without β-CD is around 20–25 % within 30 min, and the most of the drug was released within 3 h (Fig. S7). The slow release of hydrophobic drug from hydrogels with β-CD could be attributed to host–guest complex formation between ibuprofen and β-CD present on the polymer chain [73,74]. There are different factors involved in the complex formation between β-CD and ibuprofen, including (1) the van der Waals interaction between the hydrophobic moiety of the guest molecules (ibuprofen) and the β-CD cavity; (2) the hydrogen bonding between the polar functional groups of drug molecules and hydroxyl groups of β-CD; and (3) the release of high energy water molecules from the cavity during the complex formation [75]. To predict the formation of ibuprofen and β-CD inclusion complex, 1H NMR spectroscopy was employed. Fig. S8 displayed the 1H NMR spectra of β-CD, ibuprofen, and the hydrogel/ibuprofen complex. For this analysis, the ibuprofen-loaded β-CD/CMC gel was used. The 1H NMR spectrum of β-CD showed the signal from the glucopyranose units of cyclodextrins, H-1 (4.807 ppm); H-3, H-5 were located at 3.626 and 3.543 ppm, respectively. In the spectrum of ibuprofen, the signals of aromatic protons of ibuprofen appeared as two doublets corresponding to H-9 and H-5 at 7.278 and 7.265 ppm, and H-6 and H-8 at 7.180 and 7.167 ppm respectively [76]. On the investigation of 1H NMR spectrum of the hydrogel/ibuprofen complex, both aromatic protons (H-5 and H-6) of ibuprofen exhibited significant upfield displacements. Additionally, the chemical shift values of H-3 (3.617 ppm) and H-5 (3.539 ppm) shifted upfield compared to free β-CD after the inclusion of ibuprofen in cyclodextrin. The result suggested that H-3 and H-5 in cyclodextrin molecules was located inside the cyclodextrin cavity. However, the chemical shift values for H-1, H-2, H-4, and H-6 were minimal (Table S3), confirming that the drug only interacts with the inside of the cavity, as expected upon formation of an inclusion complex [77]. Therefore, the 1H NMR spectra suggest that ibuprofen and β-CD successfully formed an inclusion complex.

On the other hand, the obtained hydrogels were also sensitive to pH (Fig. 7(c)). When the pH value varied from 1.2 to 7.4, the release rate of ibuprofen improved from 58 % to 96 %. The property of different release rates in an acidic condition is a good direction to reach the specific slow release of drugs in different organisms of the human body. The possible reasons are due to the protonated effect. When the pH was less than 4, ionization of the carboxyl groups was repressed so that most of –COO− may lose negative charge in the form of -COOH that existed in the hydrogels. At a higher pH (pH = 5.0), the hydrogen bonds are broken owning to deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups, that is, -COO− groups were formed. Therefore, the electrostatic repulsion between the carboxylic acid groups is increased compared to a strongly acidic pH [78]. Consequently, there was an increased trend for the swelling capacity of the hydrogel, which made the interactions between drugs and hydrogels weak, thus further facilitating drug delivery from hydrogels. On the contrary, at pH = 7.4, that is a neutral condition, the form of -COO− didn't change and NH3 + (that is from the protonation of -NH2 in MA-CDs) occurred change turning into -NH2 groups via deprotonated process, thus increasing hydrodynamic diameter and accelerating the diffusion rate of ibuprofen from the hydrogel networks [79]. To further explore the release behavior of hydrogels and ensure successful release of ibuprofen into PBS solution, we conducted ibuprofen solubility tests under different pH conditions (Table S2). We used the maximum drug release amount of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel under optimal conditions (pH = 7.4, T = 37 °C) as a baseline to test the solubility of ibuprofen at different pH. The entire experimental process was conducted under the same conditions as the drug release test (using the same solvent volume, temperature, and duration). The results are as follows: at pH 7.4, the solubility percentage is about 56 %; at pH 5.0, the solubility percentage is about 10 %; and at pH 1.2, it is only 1.8 %. Solubility does not change over time; however, in Fig. 7(c), the drug release rate of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel varies significantly at different pH levels, with the highest slope observed at pH 7.4. The cumulative release trend of the ibuprofen is similar to that reported in previous literature [80], and lower cumulative drug release rate of ibuprofen-loaded hydrogels observed in acidic pH could be attributed to the restricted swelling capacity of the hydrogel under acidic conditions [81,82]. Our findings reveal that the hydrogel exhibits pH responsiveness. For instance, when β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel is used as a controlled-release carrier to deliver drugs to the intestine (pH = 7.4) by oral administration, effectively avoiding the absorption of drugs in the mouth (pH = 0.9–1.5) and stomach (pH = 1.5–4.0) in the process of transportation, which is conducive to improve drug concentrations at specific sites and therapeutic effects. A. Naeem et al. [14] successfully developed pH-sensitive hydrogels based on β-cyclodextrin and chitosan for oral drug delivery. Based on an in vivo test carried out using rabbits, the synthesized hydrogels have the potential to provide oral release of gallic acid under different pH conditions and there are no adverse effects on blood chemistry or histopathology of major organs. Therefore, in this way, the demand for drug delivery to specific sites can not only be realized but avoid the excessive release of drugs, which can cause toxic effects on normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility evaluation and antioxidant efficiency of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: As for medical materials, biocompatibility is a prominent factor that we should closely concentrate on. HepG2 cells were employed to investigate the cytocompatibility of β-CD/CMC gel, β-CD/CMC-PVA gel and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) via the CCK-8 method. All the synthesized bio-hydrogels demonstrated relatively low cytotoxicity, with cell viability remaining above 80 %, consistent with findings reported in previous studies [46]. The cell viability values of the β-CD/CMC, β-CD/CMC-PVA, and β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) ranging from 99 % to 87 %, 102 % to 86.5 %, 99.2 % to 82.3 %, respectively were presented in Fig. 8(c). This proves that the prepared bio-hydrogels can be excellent candidates as oral delivery systems. Notably, antibacterial property is also an important parameter for drug delivery. In the current work, the antibacterial characteristics of the hydrogels were discussed, and the results are shown in Fig. 8(a-b). Almost no colonies were observed from β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) groups after culturing 48 h and antibacterial rates were up to more than 99 %, which indicates that the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogels have excellent antibacterial properties against Gram-negative E. coli. Similarly, after 48 h co-culturing the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel with Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), no colonies grew on agar plates. This demonstrates that the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) hydrogel have the same antibacterial effect against Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus). The antibacterial mechanism may be attributed to the addition of PNIPAM. It is a cationic polymer with a positive surface charge that attracts negatively charged bacteria through electrostatic interaction, leading to bacterial death by disrupting cell membrane integrity and inactivating bacterial enzymes [83,84].

Excess free radicals can make the body more susceptible to infections by germs and bacteria, thereby increasing the prevalence rate. Therefore, the antioxidant activities of the hydrogel incorporated with ibuprofen were assessed by evaluating its scavenging efficiency for DPPH [1]. Taking the dual-network hydrogel as an example, 10 mg of the hydrogel sample was added to 10 mL of DPPH solution with different concentrations (10.00 mg/mL, 8.00 mg/mL, 6.00 mg/mL, 4.00 mg/mL, 2.00 mg/mL, and 1.00 mg/mL). The above-mixed solution was sonicated for 20 min under dark conditions and the DPPH scavenging efficiency was shown in Fig. 8(d). The hydrogel shows an increased trend in antioxidant capacity with the increase of DPPH solution concentration (DPPH scavenging efficiency >85 % at 10.00 mg/mL). It is evident from the inserted picture that the original dark purple color of the DPPH solution changed to bright yellow after the addition of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel. Briefly, β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel with superior antioxidant ability displays great potential as a smart material for sustained drug delivery.

Furthermore, for the Live/Dead assay (Fig. 8(e)), most of HepG2 cells with a spindle shape, were green in all the hydrogel groups and control groups (only culture medium) after 24 h incubation, and there were few dead cells. Based on these results, the three hydrogels have good cytocompatibility, indicating that they have great potential as drug delivery agents.

The dual-network structure β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel with the sequential interpenetrating network was successfully synthesized using MA-CDs as a crosslinking agent that owns the intrinsic fluorescent property, displaying excellent swelling and water retention capability. Meanwhile, β-CD plays an important role in improving the loading capability of hydrophobic drugs. Ibuprofen release behaviors of the β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) showed that CMC provides good protection against the stomach acidic environment. A controlled release from the synthesized hydrogel was observed that shows sensitive responses to both the pH micro-environment of the small intestine and the physiological temperature, thus can be used as the carrier of ibuprofen to precisely relieve intestinal pain. Cytotoxicity results illustrated that the prepared hydrogel exhibits relatively low toxicity toward HepG2 cells. Interestingly, the prepared β-CD/CMC-(PVA-NIPAM) gel, which possesses fluorescent properties, can be further utilized for cell imaging and drug tracking.
